summary: Drew DeVault's November 2019 status update celebrates the one-year anniversary of SourceHut's public alpha. The bulk of the update covers VR compositor work, where he and Simon Ser built wxrc, a lightweight Wayland XR Compositor based on open standards (OpenXR via Monado) rather than proprietary SteamVR. He also previews the upcoming Sway release with VNC support and input latency improvements, notes community-driven progress on aerc and ctools, and mentions writing new Wayland book chapters.
key_points: SourceHut reached its one-year alpha anniversary with a detailed master plan published for 2020
wxrc replaces the earlier Godot-based VR compositor with a bespoke C project built on open-source OpenXR (Monado) instead of proprietary SteamVR
The VR work is cross-cutting, generating patches across Mesa, Wayland, Xorg, wlroots, sway, and new Vulkan/OpenXR standards
The upcoming Sway release includes VNC support, taskbar management, input latency reductions, and drawing tablet support
aerc and ctools are growing through community contributions, with message templates and new Unix utility implementations
money_quotes: It's a bespoke C project instead of a Godot-based compositor, resulting in something much lighter weight and more efficient.
The previous incarnations were based on SteamVR, which is a proprietary runtime and proprietary API.
This is really cross-cutting work and we're making improvements across the whole graphics ecosystem to support it.
In aerc news, thanks entirely to its contributors and not to me, lots of new features have been making their way in.
Today marks the 1 year anniversary of Sourcehut opening its alpha to public registration.
key_insight: DeVault's VR compositor work exemplifies his philosophy of replacing proprietary dependencies with open-source alternatives, moving from SteamVR to OpenXR/Monado while contributing improvements across the entire open graphics stack.
